ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried royale chives; closest parent 'stir-fried food product' (FOODON_03302710) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chives (Allium schoenoprasum and related species), cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC for cooked chives used as closest parent; no specific code for stir-fried royale chives with seafood exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried royale chives as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: seasoning with oyster sauce and fish sauce
main_ingredients: royale chives (garlic chives / Chinese chives, Allium tuberosum)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried royale chives is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a shared side dish or individual plate in a Thai seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Garlic chives, cooked (SR Legacy) and Shrimp, cooked (SR Legacy); composite estimation for stir-fried chives with prawns
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried royale chives exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked garlic chives (FDC ID 169230) and cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), combined with typical stir-fry oil addition (~5 g oil per 100 g dish) and oyster/fish sauce seasoning contribution. Proportions estimated as approximately 60% chives, 25% prawns, 10% oil/sauce, 5% aromatics by weight. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant dish may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked non-starchy vegetables (GI ~15)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried royale chives. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables (GI range 10–20, representative value 15). Royale chives (Allium tuberosum) are a non-starchy vegetable with low digestible carbohydrate content, making a GI of 15 defensible. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 5.4 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.6 g, classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving of stir-fried royale chives contributes approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 42–48% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 1.6 per serving, stir-fried royale chives pose minimal postprandial glucose impact and can be included freely in a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium-rich sauces are moderated.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium from condiments offset by beneficial allicin compounds and omega-3 contribution from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the dish provides cardioprotective allicin-related compounds from garlic chives and lean protein from prawns, the high sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing steamed or lightly seasoned preparations is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Royale Chives (Thai: ผัดกุยช่ายหอม, romanized: Phat Kui Chai Hom) is a Thai stir-fried vegetable dish featuring royale chives — a cultivar of garlic chives (Allium tuberosum) prized for their tender stems, mild garlic-onion flavour, and aromatic fragrance. In Thai seafood restaurants, the dish is commonly prepared with fresh prawns or shrimp, garlic, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and white pepper, cooked rapidly over high heat in a wok to preserve the bright green colour and crisp-tender texture of the chives. The result is a savoury, slightly sweet, and umami-rich dish with a delicate crunch. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrates (approximately 5.4 g per 100 g), moderate in protein (7.2 g per 100 g) from the prawns, and provides meaningful amounts of vitamin A, vitamin C, iron, and calcium from the chives. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~15), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is elevated due to oyster sauce and fish sauce (~480 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-free in its traditional form, though oyster sauce may contain wheat. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai seafood restaurants such as Shrimp Prawn Seafood at Boat Quay, typically priced around SGD 16.80 per plate. It is commonly ordered as a shared vegetable side dish alongside rice and other seafood preparations.

royale chives: กุยช่ายหอม

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried royale chives was found in any primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for component ingredients (cooked garlic chives and cooked shrimp) with standard stir-fry oil addition. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked garlic chives (FDC ID 169230) and cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), with an estimated 5 g vegetable oil addition per 100 g of finished dish and oyster/fish sauce sodium contribution. No exact match was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or other primary regional databases. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for garlic chives or shrimp used as the basis for this estimate. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source data for the component ingredients — no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables; no direct GI measurement for royale chives exists in the published literature. 5. The 'Royale Chives' designation used by the restaurant likely refers to a premium cultivar of Allium tuberosum (garlic chives); nutritional values are treated as equivalent to standard garlic chives. 6. Halal status is marked false because fish sauce and oyster sauce sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without restaurant-specific verification. 7. Gluten-free status is marked false because commercial oyster sauce typically contains wheat starch. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from the fatty acid profile of vegetable oil (assumed sunflower or palm oil blend) used in stir-frying.
